CURRENT KFFAIRS,

To the infliction of policemen’s testimonials there is
no end.  Tallaght, in the County Dublin, is widely
known in connection with an episode in '67. It is just
now notorious as the scene of one of those many pests
that inflict Ireland—a threatened outbreak of a testi-
monial for a retired peeler. We have already a song,
“Tho Peeler and the Goat.”” Who will write a few catchy
verses about “The Peeler and the Testimonial?”’ ~ One
Sergeant, James Long, a “minion’’ of b}m British law,
has  rotired from “active” service at Tal-
laght; whilst on “active’’ service we do not know
whether he specialised in fishing for trout, concertina
playing, creasing his hair, or putting on polish
_ on his boots; but anyway he has left his onerous and

nation-making job, and has gono into private life. The
inevitable touting circular states:— Sergeant James
Long, R.I.C., who has been for many years in charge
of Tallaght Station, and has always proved himself &
‘worthy and efficient officer, and endeared himself
by his kindly disposition and courtesy, in this and the
adjoining localities, having now retired, We, tho under-
signed, have formed a Committee to present him with
. suitablo testimonial as a mark of the esteem in which
~ he is held. We ask you for a contribution towards
- game.”’ y
are “We, the undersigned,”” do you think?

' all, there is Dr. Poett, St. Dominick’s,

Tallaght. He is a dispensary doctor in the employ-
ment of the South Dublin Union; next comes Jas.
Jolley, one of the “Saved’’ from Kilatown ; then ther_e
is P. Doran, Roso Cottage, a “Nationalist’”” who is
anxious to get up a collection for this retired “minion’
of tho British Government! We suppose if P. Doran
was marching behind a fife and drum band with a
groen sash slung round him, and facing for the Pheenix
Park to attend a U.L.L. meeting, no adjective in the
British language would be sanguinary enough for his
taste for “‘the foul minions of the nefarious Govern-
ment.”” Mr, Delany, of Greenhills, is another “Nationa-
list.”” Whatever may be said of the hills where he
lives, he apparently is a very green Nationalist, any-
way. The remaining “We, the undersigned,”’ are two
“Saved,”” one R. Boardman and a C. Barrett. Why
cannot this poor bobby be allowed to “move on’’ in peace
without these touting circulars being sent out to the
people of Tallaght district? Have not these people
something else to do with their money than firing it
into the hat of a man who was paid by the Government,
and is now pensioned off? Our advice to the people is
to do what they have a legal right to do—keep their
money in their pockets.

We look forward to throwing an enquiring eye over
the list of subscribers. When the dispensary doctor and
several green nationalists—one of them a Bung who
keeps the “ Cuckoo "’—are on the Committee, we may
reasonably look forward to entertainment in perusing
the mere generous donors of a modest sixpence and up-
wards.  This Tallaght testimonial to a “minion’’ of
the British law is exceptionally interesting. Policeman
Long, we understand, has been doing his nation-making
work at Tallaght for some dozen years or so. We would
like to know how many undetected crimes were com-
mitted in the district during the last dozen years. We
have heard that various houses had been broken into,
and at least on one occasion the  Idolatrous’’ Domini-
can Church was the object of one or more house-breakers’
attentions. How many of these criminals did the local
Sherlock Holmes, now happily retired on pension, track
down? Where is the retired nation-builder that the
green proprietor of the “ Cuckoo ’’ bungery is anxious
to testimonial (if we may coin a verb) residing at
present? There was a house or cottage condemned by
the local sanitary authorities, and we have heard that
his Excellency the Sergeant is now living in that place!
Have the sanitary authorities seen that the place has
been properly overhauled and taken out of the state in
which it was when the sanitary authorities condemned
it? We will keep our eye on Tallaght; it is an interest-
ing place with historic associations.

We take the following from the Irish Times:—
“Wanted immediately, clerk in Land Agent’s country
Offico, with good experienco of work required; un-
married ; salary £72; I.C. Apply, with references, Adair, '
Ballynoe, Tullow.”” So Mr. Adair’s clerk must be
“Baved.” Who is Mr. Adair? We see that there is
a John Olphert Adair, of Ballynoe, Tullow, a magis-
trate of the County of Carlow. How is it that there
is no outery against this magistrate publicly flaunting
his exclusive dealing and boycotting in the public press.

Mr. Jeremiah MacVeagh appears to be one of the
most efficient and active members of the Irish Parlia-
montary Party. We are glad to seo that ho has, and
apparently with success, interested himself in the Dub-
lin, Wicklow and Wexford Railway Company, and that
a scheme for competitive examination for clerkships is
being arranged. We trust that he will be able to induce
the_du'ecbors to make the Irish language a compulsory
subject of the examination. Mr. MacVeagh is
keeping a sharp look-out—and they want sharp lo
after—on the designs of the directors of the Gr

|
|

|
17

Northern Bigot Railway. He has given notice of oppo-
sition to the second reading of a Bill being-promoted in
the interests of that bigoted Corporation. He has done
so very properly with a view to inducing the Great
Northern Company to throw open their clerkships to
compstition. Amongst other members who have also
put down notices in opposition to this Bill are:—Mr.
Hazleton, Mr. W. Redmond, Mr. J. O’Mara, Mr. Bo-
land, Mr. Thomas O’Donnell, and Mr, V. Kennedy. We
seo that Mr. Pat O’'Brien is also.interesting himself in
the matter, but had %e not better give all his precious
attention and commanding ability to backing up the
Bung spongers on St. Patrick?

What answer has the “sympathetic’”” Mr. Bryce and
the soft-spoken Lord Aberdeen to make to the prosecu-
tion of Mr. P. O’Carroll, T.C., for having his name in
Irish only on his cart? Mr. Drury fined the perpetrator
of this terrible crime 10s., and held that he was bound
by the decision of the King’s Bench. No doubt the
Gaelic League made a tactical mistake in bringing the
question involved in Irish names on carts before the
King’s Bench, as every magistrate has now the excuse
of that judgment. However, at this time of day the
question as to whether magistrates are bound by that
decision or not, is only a minor point; the chief ques-
tion is—why are prosecutions undertaken now that the
so-called “Liberal’”” Government are in office? Why, it
was only the other day that the “sympathetic’’ Mr.
Bryce was talking sweet nothings down in Kilkenny
about the language movement ; and if we recollect aright
Lady Aberdeen replied in Irish to some telegram of con-
gratulation or welcome in Irish which some busybody
or other sent her. In face of all this a man is prose-
cuted for having his name in Irish only on his cart in
Dublin, and the goods of Domnall Ua Duacalla,
of Maynooth, were seized a few days ago on the head of
a fine for a similar crime. Had Mr. Bryce his tongue
in his cheek at Kilkenny? If not, what explanation
has he to offer for this latest prosecution?  TIs this
governing Ireland according to Irish idcas?

The I.A.O.S., it appears, from a reply which the
“sympathetic’’ Mr, Bryce gave Mr. Dillon, had, up to
tho 28th February last, received the enormous sum of
£18,966 from the Department (Scotch). The ‘“‘sober
Nationalist’’ who draws a salary from the I.A.O.S. is
a great admirer, and a thick-and-thin champion of poor
Sir Horace Plunkett. Mr. P. J. Hannon, one of the
ablest men of the I.A.0.S. that received £19,000 from
the Department (Scotch), has gone to South Africa.
But then Mr. Hannon was an ‘“Idolator.”” When the
“Saved’’ get the fat jobs, we are told that there are no
compatent, “Idolators’”” to be found. It is curious that
when South Africa wanted a man they did not take out
the “sober Nationalist,”” or any others of the dividend-
paying way of thinking that have soft “livings’’ in the
I.A.0.S. and the Department. If Mr, Hannon had
been “Saved,”” we have no doubt that the Department
would have retained his services by outbidding the South
African authorities. It will be remembered that at
first Mr. Hannon was only “lent’’ to South Africa.
Probably the gang who rule the Department and the
I.A.0.S. were only too anxious to geb rid of an able
and masterful “Idolator’” at any cost. By the way,
in what University was the “sober Nationalist”
trained?

As the I.A.0.8. has reccievd about £19,000 out of
the public funds, perhaps some member of the Irish
Party would ask for a return of the names, occupations
and salaries and other emoluments of all those in ser-
vice at 22 Lincoln Place.

‘Who is Darmot Chenevix Trench? Ile writes a letter
anyway to Alf Foz in connection with a particular pre-
sentation, and in the body of the letter he informs the
reader of Alf that subscriptions may be forwarded to
“Dermot; Chenevix Trench, #sq.”’ The italics are ours.
Might not Mr. D. C. Trench have left it to the choice
of his possible correspondents whether they would affix
“Esq.’” or not to his name? Would plain A/7. Dermot
Chenevix Trench be regarded as an insult?

TIIE LEADER.

May 19, 1906.

Mr. Stephen Gwynn is one of the few Protestants in
this country who graciously condescends to be an Irish-
man and a Nationalist. Being a non-Catholic Nationa-
list educated at Oxford, or some place like that, he is,
of course, a “superior’’ person. He wrote a charac-
teristic lotter to the IFreeman last week. Mr. Gwyni
is concerned that tho Irish Parliamentary Party, of
which ho aspired to be a member, should not become
Catholic first and Nationalist afterwards, So well do
the few Protestants who become Nationalists play the
watch-dog on behalf of “ uon»scctaria,m:sm,“' tl}a.t were
thero no such thing as a Protestant Nationalist it would
bo an effective, if unscrupulous, stroke of policy for the
Protestants to put up two or three of their number to
play the part of Nationalists so as to weaken the cffec-
tivo force of the Nationalists .

Wo don’t find Mr. Alfred Webb or Mr. BSte-
phen Gwynn keeping up a constant fire on their
co-religionists who, as . Bngland’s Faithful Gar-
rison, as Ireemasons, as Orangemen, as highly-
paid officials in Trinity College and other public depart-
ments of profit, are such a curse to this country; but
having graciously condescended to become Nationalists,
they are sure to bark at the slightest tendency that they
may think the Party shows of straying from the true
fold of “non-sectarianism’’ Mr. Stephen Gwynn, like
Sir Horace Plunkett and the Rev. Bigot of West-
port, is a very superior person, and, no doubt, many
of the self-distrusting mere Irish open their mouths and
tako him at his own valuation. ~Mr. Gwynn says:—
“But at the same time we (Protestant Nationalists)
trust that the National Party will not emphasise its
sectarian character by ncedless crusading in whatever
is presented as a Catholic cause.”” Might we, crawling
Irish Catholic Nationalists, be permitted to trust that
Protestant Nationalists like Mr. Stephen Gwynn should
turn to their own co-religionists in Trinity College and
elsewhere in TIreland, who are anti-Irish, and
endeavour to influence them? There is something
impudent, to use the mildest word, in the airs which tha
Gwynns and the Plunketts and such like give themselves
when they condescend to take an interest in this country
upon which, through no particular merits of their own,
they and theirs have fattened, and have grown bump-
tious.

The following advertisement appeared in the London
Daily Telegraph of May Tth :— ‘Treland.—Wanted, for
gentleman’s country house (lady English), two expe-
riencad, recommended Protestant servants. Cook
(dairy), housemaid (assist parlourmaid), both useful.
carly risers, clean, tall, and capable. Ages 25. Wages
£18 and £16, found; no beer. Apply B., Coolmelagh,
Newtownbarry.”” So the English Protestant servants
will get “no beer.”” Who is B., Coolmelagh, Newtown-
barry? Wo find that one of the magistrates in the
County of Wexford is a Colonel Thomas Braddell, Cool-
melagh, Newtownbarry.

L

A new shop has been opened in Armagh, in which
everything manufactured in Ireland in the book, sta-
tionery and fancy line is to get a fair show. The pro-
prietor, Mr. Short, was anxious to stock everything that
might be required for the “ National’’ schools. One
of tho publishing houses in Dublin refused to supply
My, Short with their books, as they alveady had an
account in Armagh. When their representative would
visit Armagh next autumn they would instruct him to
go info the matter if Mr. Short was still anxious to
stock their books. Mr. Short pointed out in reply, that
he wanted their publications for the convenience of his
customers amongst the teachers, and that he was pre-
pared to sond cash with order. Mr. Short, and wo
think naturally under the circumstances, wrote sorie-
what hotly concerning what he called “an unfrir
monopoly.”  The firm retorted, under date April 30th,
that they had already three accounts in Armagh, and
that no question of monopoly arose, and wound up—
“The threatening tone of your letter precludes us from
c_ons.xdermg your request further, and we must now
decline to open an account with you.”” This appears to
us a rather curious way of doing business. Surely a

once told the world that he came of a squireen stock,
and this fact induces us to give one more quotation
from Goldwin Smith’s book :—“From the ranks of the
middlemen were partly drawn the upstart gentry, or
squireens—a  roistering, debauched, drinking and
duelling crew, whose tyrannical insolence scandalised
Arthur Young, ruling with the horse-whip a peasantry
cowering under the lash, and hopeless of redress by the
Jlaw.”” The pride of ancestry is strong in the Trinity
College professor.

This rather dull and unilluminating book is interest-
ing just as Sir Horace Plunkett’s book was interesting—
it throws back a searchlight on a type of narrow, shal-
low and bigoted mind which, by force of characteristic,
British impudence and self-complacency is fully con-
vinced that it is nothing if not broad and deep. The
Goldwin Smiths, and people of that ilk, do not enjoy a
“walk-over’’ at this time of day.

furr-c- DOWN WITH SECRET SOCIETIES.
‘|'S it not time that Irish Catholics rose up and pro-
tested effectively against secret societies in Ireland?
‘We do not mean secret political societies of a Nationalist
tendency, for these'are very well denounced in pastoral
and pulpit. We refer to the secret societies amongst
England’s Faithful Garrison in this country—the Free-
masons and the Orangemen. As “Idolators’ are warned
against secret societies, why should they not protest
against non-“Idolators’’ banding themselves together in
such bodies? If we “Idolators’ play the game in the
open, why allow to go unchallenged those who play it
in the secret lodges? Why should not a move be made
to discontinue Sir William Goulding as Chairman of the
Great Southern and Western Railway on the simple
ground that he is “Grand Steward”’ of the secret
plotters by the name of the Free and Accepted Masons
of Ireland? What confidence can any outsider have in
such an official? Our readers will remember Mr. Jim
Campbell’s harangues and slanders on the Catholic As-
sociation. That gentleman knows not only the power
of open organisation, but of secret organisation also,
for he is now “ Grand Sword-bearer ’’ of the Freemasons
in this country. Sir James Creed Meredith is a highly
paid Secretary of the Royal University of Ireland, and
he is also “Deputy Grand Master’’ of the secret con-
spiracy that goes by the name of the Freemasons. There
has been a lot of talk about the undergraduates who
stormed an organ on a recent occasion, but why do not
some of the Senate object to one of the secretaries being
the “Deputy Grand Master’’ of the Freemasons? One
James M. Lowry is “Senior Grand Deacon’’ of the Free-
masons, and a man of the same name and initials is
Assistant Clerk of the Judgment, Record and Writs
Office. If the Assistant Clerk of that office were known
to be a Fenian, would he be tolerated in it? The “Grand
Treasurer’’ of this secret pest is one W. J. C. Crawley,
who runs some sort of an academy in Ely Place, where
George the “Saved’’ lives. One M. E. Solomons now
wields the “Inner Guard Sword’’ that once flashed like
burning acid in the hand of Brother Goulding, “ gentle-
man,” and M. E. Solomons is Vice-Consul for the
Austro-Hungarian Empire; George Scriven, M.D., of
33 Stephen’s Green North, is “Junior Grand Deacon ;’’
Thomas R. G. Joze, the musiciman, is “Grand
Organist;’’ J. Fox Goodman, Master of the Crown
Office, Four Courts, is “Grand Director of Ceremonies;’’
Lord Castletown is “ Grand Secretary '’ of the Secret
Now, why should all these and the others be allowed
to play their games behind the hedges of a secreb
society ! After all, ““Idolators’’ cannot be expected to
keep on fighting with sugar-sticks all the time. If we
must keep on fighting with sugar-sticks, perhaps we

might as well clear out of the country sooner rather
than later. If men like Brother Goulding, and Brother
Meredith, and Brother Castletown, and Brother Jim
Campbell can hit the “ Idolators’ from behind a hedge,
why should not tho “Idolators’ stand up in the open,
and, at least, attack the hedge? Surely thero is nothing
intolerant or lacking in equity in suggesting that all
members of secret societies like the Freemason pest and
the Orange atrocity should be excluded from all posi-
tions of trust, honour and emolument in the country.

“Down with sceret societies” 1is a cry that cven
Charles E, Mar Lsq., D.L., the “educated’’ Catholic,
can scarcely find ground to oppose. Are wo to waib
until the Freemazons have done in Ireland what they are
reputed to have done in France until we rouso ourselves
to the realitics of the situation? It is, in most cases,
impossible to know who are Freemasons, bub surely, on
tho principlo that no person who is a member of that
pernicious secret pest should be clected to any position
of trust or power in a country where the majority play
the game in the open, candidates for all offices should
be required to declare, if called upon, whether or not
they were engaged in that secret and pernicious
conspiracy.

CONFUSION OF THOUGHT IN BRITAIN.

T HE second reading of the Education Bill is passed

by even more than the expected majority. It
is two to ome for secularism—or “‘simple Bible teach-
ing;”’ it is all the same to us Catholics. Upon the
Bill and its provisions I do not want to say anything;
T only wish to indulge in a few reflections upon matters
arising out of the vast discussions on the Bill.

In the discussions outside the House of Commons one
could not help noticing the incessant recurrence of the
word “dogma.’” It occurred most frequently in tho
speeches of the extreme Dissenters, and those who used
it spoke in the manner of men who conceive dogma to
be some kind of soul-slaying superstition, associated in
a peculiar degree with “Romanism”—and therefore all
the more soul-slaying. ~ What, after all, is dogma!
Thoss last resources of the perplexed mind, the dic-
tionaries, simply say that it is a settled opinion, a fixed
view. Of course, your anti-dogmatist says that dogma
“stifles thought ;’’ hence Protestants can have nothing
to do with anything so Romanist and “medieval.”” By
the way, if you want to damn anything just now, you
have only to call it medizval; on the other hand, claim
that a view is “modern,’”’ and it is supposed thereby to
be rendered indisputable. Things are not now argued
squarely upon their merits, but simply with reference
to whether they are “in harmony with modern ideas”
or not. As the Catholic Church is not a mushroom
institution, it cannot possibly fulfil the all-essential con-
dition of being “modern,”” and so it is put into the doclk
at once! This plan of assessing the value of ideas, not
upon their truth, but upon their relation to modernity,
issurely a new organon of intellectual judgment. Dogma,
being a striking element, in the Catholic Church, is not
a “modern idea.”” It is supposed to “cramp the mind,”
and “hinder free inquiry;’’ hence it must be bad. Yet
is there not dogma, or settled opinion, underlying all
civilisation? What is the doctrine that “no man shall
be a judge in his own cause *’ but a settled opinion, any
attempt to upset which must cause alarm at once? “No
taxation without representation’’ is a dogma of the Bri-
tish Constiution. Habeas corpus, or the principle that
no man shall be kept in jail without a trial, is'another ;
Trial by Jury is another; Freedom' of Parliament
js another, as Charles I. found' when he: tried
to arrest the Five Members. One king' lost his
head because he had not sufficient respect for these
dogmas of the British' Constitution, while another' lost
his crown. These principles are “settled opinions!’—very
much settled, indeed—whereon no dispute is permitted,
save under pain of civil war or revolution. How:
strange, then, that while “fixed views!’ are necessary'
as the basis of ani earthly constitution, they: should be
so soul-slaying as the basis of religion!' ~ Surely the
dogma of not enduring anybody: else’s: dogma is the'

THE LEADER.

May 19, 1906.

worst of all dogmas. The dogma that all dogma is
wrong, is the most false and_ stupid dogma of all.
Without some settled opinions we must have moral,
mental, civil and religious anarchy ; yet dogma, ;i‘,' ﬁlxed
viow, is spoken of by some as if it were somc“ ind of
withering superstition, instead of bm'),lg §{Jme hing as
nec:ssary as air or water. True, cvery. sofitled _‘)Pmm“
is nob necessarily true, just as all air s not pure nor all
water good. The Protestant Succession to. the Crown
is, since 1689, a dogma of the British Constitution; yeb
no Catholic can give any spiritual assent to it, though
morally and legally he may acquiesco in nvt for the sake
of proventing useless civil convulsions. The first four
words of tho Apostles’ Creed are a dogma which T sup-
poso oven members of the “Free” Churches, and pos-
sibly Dr. Clifford himself, would assent to; ya»t when
it comes to having a rap at “Rome’’ all dogma is anathe-
matised as something medieval and unclean. So much
for dogma.

Another thing which was eternally cropping up was
that innocent phrase, “simple Bible teaching.”” Now,
just an anccdote. A pious lady was once contending
with Dr. Johnson about religious affairs; she spoke as
if she regarded the Biblo as the grand and easy arbiter
of all difficulties. “Madam,” said the downright John-
son, “the Bible is the most difficult book in the world.””
Without any reserves, I believe it is. Yet in one re-
spect it is the very easiest ; it is the easiest to misinter-
pret. Nevertheless, this is the book which is to be the
subject of simple teaching, by a body of expounders,
some of whom, for certain, will be non-believers in it!
The idea of giving simple expositions of the Pentateuch,
the Prophetical Books, and the Psalms, is one which
could only enter the heads of people who have entirely
novel notions of what constitutes simplicity. What T
suspect will happen is this: snippets from the Bible,
mostly perhaps from the New Testament, will be read,
sans comment. This will be the teaching! If this be
so, as curious a notion of what constitutes “teaching’’
is predicated, as of what constitutes “‘simplicity.””

An odd spectacle was afforded by Mr. Bryce’s inter-
vention in the debate on the Education Bill.  This
gentleman I understand to be a Unitarian.  Now,
neither my own Church nor the Anglican body would
regard a Unitarian as a Christian at all. ' Hence the
sight of the Right Honourable James Bryce expounding
the Christianity of the first three centuries must have
been diverting to the Catholics and High Churchmen
present. “TIs there no such thing as what is called our
common Christianity ?’’ asked the ex-professor. There
is, certainly, but some of us feel shy of accepting non-
Christians as expounders of it. Another British states-
man, Mr. Lloyd-George, argues the multiplicity of Non-
conformist Churches as a reason why universal denomi-
nationalism is not practicable. ~For this beautiful
reason the Catholic Church, which has succeeded in
preserving its unity, is to be penalised! Was ever such
a doctrine heard before? To be penalised for not
splitting into pieces!

The only ground for satisfaction, so far, is the excel-
lent manner in which the Irish Party presented the
Catholic case, and the frank recognition of that fact
by the British press, Tory and Radical. Mr. Healy’s
speech in particular was fine. As a Catholic party, our
Parliamentarians have made their best sucoess, it seems
to me. TnAAL.

Or will they in this as in other matters—perhaps owing
to mental habits acquired during two hundred years of
exclusion from all participation in public affairs (as
“8.”” says in your issue of April 7th) remain careless
and negligent ?

Surely the time has arrived for some marked effects
of the Local Government Act upon the national charac-
ter for energy and enterprise to have made their appear-
ance, to say nothing of other influences. Now it hap-
pens very fortunately that the founding of a new fire
insurance office, so far as the question of capital is con-
cerned, is greatly simplified by the fact that insurance
business of every description differs from other business
in one mighty particular. It is the customers who give
the credit. Premiums are invariably payable in ad-
vance no matter for what purpose; fire premiums usually
a whole year in advance. On the other hand, claims
for fire losses are not payable in forty-eight hours, or
within any definite period. Where the claim is a large
or an involved one, many months may elapse before the
settlement is effected; an interval during which, of
course, first premiums are received from new insurers,
and further premiums from old ones. Then again, the
premises, and the property in them, are, in an immense
majority of instances, over-insured—a circumstance
which greatly swells the premium income without add-
ing to the risk. Owing to these and other considera-
tions innumerable the capital necessary to float a fire
insurance company need only be trifling, except for the
purpose of acquiring a connection by purchase, or of
creating one as the result of salaries, travelling expenses,
extensive advertising, and other such influences. In
the latter case the result is always dangerously uncer-
tain, and success hardly ever immediate.  The new
company has to push its way into the charmed circle of
the old and wealthy ones, paying heavily for its pro-
gress step by step, and also for its experience; and not
unfrequently courting disaster by having to accept the
“leavings’’ of the well-established offices, hoping to be
in a position to refuse to renew them before catastrophies
occur.

In the case of an influential connection existing ready
to hand, whether in the shape of a learned pro-
fession, a great trading influence, or a religious
denomination, the company established to * work
it, besides being free from the above-mentioned
perils and pitfalls, requires very Uitle capital. Such a
company is in the fortunate position of the unlicensed
- trader who is so lucky as to get a licence from the Re-
corder on his first application, instead of having to pay
_ four or five thousand pounds for one to some licensed
- neighbour. Should the Licensed Grocers and Vintners

THE GAELIC ATHLETIC ASSOCIATION.

A Capa,—I have read with interest the very able
article in last week’s issue, from the pen of “ Observer,””
regarding the above organisation, and while not in ac-
cord with all the statements made in the anticle, still T
feel bound to admit that a good deal of them are too
true. In the first place, your correspondent refers to
the financial position of the Association, and refers to
the fact that the G.A.A. possesses no ground of its own.
The tone of your correspondent’s remarks would lead one
to believe that the present members of the different
Councils and Boards are to blame for this state of
affairs. Such is not a fact. It is the old Governing
Body in the past that is responsible for the present
state of the G.A.A. Years ago, when the Association
was in a prosperous state, no effort was made by the
then Central Council to provide suitable ground. Had
they but made the slightest effort, they would now be:
the possessors of Clonturk Park, or some of the other
suitable venues in the suburbs. They were “penny
wise and pound foolish.””  Your correspondent also.
makes the astounding statement that *there is no
proper check on the gate receipts and further, that he
has “been frequently struck with the extraordinary
arrangements whereby an official of the Association is
allowed to take the gate money without any tickets
being given in return, or @ turnstile check.”” As an
official of a minor branch of the G.A.A., T feel bound
to say that it must have been in the “old days’’ when
your correspondent saw this arrangement. If not, I

‘““ extraordinary arrangement,’”’ as this statement, if
allowed to pass unnoticed, would cast much discredit on.
many of the earnest young men who are now endeavour-
ing to ropair the errors of the past, and who are try-
ing to place the Association on the same footing it occu-
pied in the early Eighties. Your correspondent also
asks, “ what has become of the money which should have-
accumulated during all those years,’” and he answers this.
question by saying “there must have been gross
mis-management, or the Association would have been
in a very strong financial position, as well as having:
numerous grounds fully equipped.” This is, unfor-
tunately, too true.  “ Gross mis-management ’’ has.
been the bane of the Association in the past, but dur-
ing the last fow years the present Central Council have
improved matters considerably, and have paid off six
or seven hundred pounds debts due owing to the « gr

I am not to be taken as complimenting the present
governing body. I admit there is room for much more
lmprovement, but I am afraid this will never be accom-
'pllshed. until the vanious clubs throughout the country
Tecognise the importance of electing capable and carnest
Tepresentatives on the different Councils, men who will
have the courage of their convictions and who will not
be afraid to give expression to them when the occasion
+demands. There are at prosent on the Central and Pro-
vincial Councils many members who only attend fix-
tures and meetings when their own particular club,
parish, or county is intevested, with the result that the
work of tho Councils is thrown on the shoulders of
a few. This is a common occurrence in connection with
the fixtures of the Councils, and the sooner it is reme-
died, the better it will be for the Association.
. Your correspondent also asks, “‘is it possiblo that there
is mot sufficient business capacity among the Gaels of
Ireland to urge them to raise enough capital to pur-
chase one good ground,” and he incidentally refers
to Jones’ Road. Might I point out to your corres-
.pondent that the Co. Dublin Board entered into nego-
tiations regarding the purchase of the ground, and
offered a sum for same some months ago when this
proparty was in the market. Since then, I understand,
the Dublin Board has secured a good ground at Inchi-
core, and it is to be earnestly hoped that the Sab-
batarians will not resort to the same ftrick by which
“they deprived the same body of Gaelic Park, Terenure,

Your correspondent suggests that the G.A.A. should
take a leaf out of the book of the Bohemian “ Soccer

'did with Dalymount Park. I am afraid this would be

restore the Association to its rightful position in the
